word_studies.summary: Adjective from aion (G165). 71 tokens in 69 verses across 19 books. Rendered 'eternal' (46.4%) and 'everlasting' (30.4%). Dominant usage: 'eternal life' (~43/69 verses). Also applied to punishment, fire, redemption, Spirit, covenant. The adjective is NT-only but LXX uses it for H5769 olam. 2 Tim 1:9 'before eternal times' proves aionios cannot mean merely 'age-long.'
word_studies.relevance: Central to Objections 1, 7, 12. DeSilva's 'age-long' translation breaks at 2 Tim 1:9 and Matt 25:46.

word_studies.summary: Verb from phobos (G5401). 90 unique verses, 110 tokens across 14 books. Nearly all forms middle/passive (deponent). Present vs. aorist imperative pattern: 'Fear not' = present imperative (me phobou, stop fearing); Rev 14:7 phobethete = aorist passive imperative (decisive call to action). Three Louw-Nida domains: Fear/Terror (025.022), Worship/Reverence (053.007), Honor/Status (087.002). Same word used for terror AND reverence — context determines sense.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 5. The phobeo word field spans terror to reverence. Rev 14:7's aorist imperative commands decisive reverential action.

word_studies.summary: Noun from phobeo. Fear/terror/reverence. In 1 John 4:18, the phobos that love casts out is qualified by kolasis (punishment-fear). In 1 Pet 1:17, believers live 'in phobos' — reverential awe coexisting with Father relationship.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 5. The noun phobos is ambiguous without context; 1 John 4:18 specifies kolasis-fear.

word_studies.summary: Punishment/correction. Appears only 2x in NT (Matt 25:46, 1 John 4:18). Both in judgment contexts. Root idea: pruning/corrective discipline (from kolazo). In 1 John 4:18, kolasis qualifies the fear that love casts out — punishment-dread, not reverential awe.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 5. The kolasis qualifier narrows 1 John 4:18's 'fear' to punishment-dread specifically.

word_studies.summary: Commandment/injunction. Feminine noun from entellomai (G1781). 65 unique verses, 71 tokens. Rendered 'commandment(s)' in 93.8% of verses. Johannine concentration: John + 1 John + 2 John = 22 verses (34%). Semantic categories: Decalogue/Mosaic law, Christ's new commandment, Father's commandment to Christ. Three NT test cases proving entole refers to Decalogue: Rom 7:7-12 (tenth commandment, 5x), Eph 6:2 (fifth commandment), Luke 23:56 (Sabbath).
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objections 4, 6. Demolishes the claim that entole cannot mean Decalogue. Nomos never appears in Revelation.

word_studies.summary: Law/Torah. Masculine noun. The standard word for the Mosaic law as a body. NEVER appears in Revelation. The entole/nomos distinction is crucial: Revelation uses entole exclusively for commandments, not nomos.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 4. Nomos absence from Revelation makes the Tinker/Answering Adventism claim (entole = Jesus' teachings, nomos = Law) irrelevant — Revelation's only vocabulary is entole.

word_studies.summary: Faith/trust/faithfulness. Feminine noun from peitho (G3982). 244 NT occurrences across 21 books. Rendered 'faith' in 68.9%. Overwhelmingly Pauline (Romans 40, Galatians 22, Hebrews 32). Semantic categories: active trust in God, body of Christian belief, faithfulness/reliability. The pistin Iesou (Rev 14:12) genitive is debated: subjective (Jesus' faithfulness), objective (faith in Jesus), or plenary (both).
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 4. The pistis Iesou genitive in Rev 14:12 — plenary reading supported by grammar-reference/greek/subjective-objective-genitive.md.

word_studies.summary: To make/do. The verb used in Rev 14:7 (poiesanti) for creation, mapping to Hebrew asah (H6213) in Exo 20:11. Every other creation text in Revelation uses ktizo (G2936). The verb switch is the diagnostic allusion to the fourth commandment.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 6. The poieo/ktizo verb switch is the strongest evidence for the Sabbath allusion.

word_studies.summary: Sabbath-keeping/Sabbath rest. NT hapax legomenon (Heb 4:9 only). Deliberately chosen over katapausis (G2663) used throughout Heb 4. Denotes Sabbath-observance in every extant Greek source. Even anti-Sabbatarian Lincoln concedes the word's Sabbath-keeping meaning.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 6. The NT hapax for Sabbath-keeping.

word_studies.summary: Judgment/judicial evaluation. Feminine noun from krino (G2919). 47 NT occurrences across 13 books. Rendered 'judgment' in 55%. Concentrated in Matthew (12) and John (11). Semantic categories: day of judgment (eschatological), judicial process, divine evaluation. Distinguished from katakrima (G2631, condemnation/adverse verdict). Rev 14:7 uses krisis — evaluation, not condemnation.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 8. The krisis/katakrima distinction is decisive: believers face evaluation but not condemnation.

word_studies.summary: Condemnation/adverse verdict with execution. Neuter noun. Only 3 NT occurrences: Rom 5:16, 5:18, 8:1. All in Romans. Kata- (against) + krima (judgment) = judgment AGAINST someone. Rom 8:1 negates katakrima for those in Christ Jesus — no condemnation, not no evaluation.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 8. Rom 8:1 negates katakrima (condemnation), NOT krisis (evaluation).

word_studies.summary: Age/perpetuity/forever. Masculine noun. The eis aionas aionon formula (into ages of ages) appears 21x in NT. Applied to God's existence, praise, and to the torment formula in Revelation.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 7. The eis aionas aionon formula in Rev 14:11 matches Rev 19:3 (Babylon's cessation) and Rev 4:8 (living creatures' praise).

word_studies.summary: Forever/eternity/perpetuity. Hebrew temporal term. Used in Isa 34:10 for Edom's 'forever' destruction that was clearly temporal. The OT prophetic convention: owlam describes permanence of result, not necessarily infinite duration of process.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 7. The OT 'forever' language for temporal destructions establishes the convention Rev 14:11 draws on.

word_studies.summary: Sign/token/mark. Hebrew noun. The Sabbath is called owth four times (Exo 31:13, 31:17, Ezek 20:12, 20:20). No other Decalogue commandment receives this designation. Rom 4:11 bridges owth/semeion to sphragis (seal).
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 10. Sabbath as uniquely designated owth among the Ten Commandments.

word_studies.summary: To seal/stamp. Verb form of sphragis (G4973). Used for the sealing of believers by the Holy Spirit (Eph 1:13, 4:30) and the sealing of God's servants (Rev 7:3-4).
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 10. The Spirit is the sealing AGENT; the seal has content.

word_studies.summary: Seal/signet/stamp. Feminine noun. 16 NT occurrences. Overwhelmingly Revelation (13/16 = 81%). Semantic categories: authentication/certification (Rom 4:11, 1 Cor 9:2), divine concealment (Rev 5:1-2), judgment unveiling (Rev 6), divine ownership/protection (Rev 7:2, 9:4). Rom 4:11 is critical — Paul calls circumcision BOTH semeion AND sphragis, breaking the vocabulary barrier.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 10. The sign/seal vocabulary bridge via Rom 4:11.

word_studies.summary: Mark/stamp/engraving. Neuter noun from charasso (to scratch/engrave). 9 NT occurrences: 8 in Revelation, 1 in Acts 17:29. Semantic categories: engraved artwork (Acts 17:29), compulsory mark (Rev 13:16-17), mark linked to worship (Rev 14:9, 11), basis for judgment (Rev 16:2, 19:20). The mark is paired with proskyneo (worship) in every Revelation occurrence — receiving the mark IS an act of false worship.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 10. The charagma is the direct antithesis of the sphragis — both placed on foreheads, but one denotes divine ownership and the other bestial allegiance.

word_studies.summary: To commit harlotry/be unfaithful. Hebrew verb. 107 OT occurrences. In the prophets, used almost exclusively for entities that were ONCE faithful (Israel, Judah, Jerusalem). The harlotry imagery requires prior covenant relationship with YHWH.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 13. The zanah vocabulary requires prior covenant — Rome never had one.

word_studies.summary: Fornication/harlotry. Greek noun. The LXX equivalent of Hebrew zanah in prophetic contexts. Used in Revelation for Babylon's fornication with the kings of the earth.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 13. The porneia of Babylon = covenant unfaithfulness, not generic immorality.

word_studies.summary: To mix/confuse. Hebrew verb. Etymology behind 'Babel/Babylon.' The essential nature of Babylon is MIXTURE — blending truth with error, sacred with profane.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 13. Babylon's essence is mixture of truth and error — fits a corrupted religious system.

word_studies.summary: To worship/bow down. Verb. The central worship verb in Rev 14:7 (proskynēsate, aorist active imperative). Commands worship of the Creator vs. beast (Rev 14:9). The charagma is linked to proskyneo in every Revelation occurrence.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objections 6, 14. The worship command in Rev 14:7 echoes the creation language of Exo 20:11.

word_studies.summary: To bear tidings/announce good news. Hebrew verb. Maps to LXX euangelizo in 18/21 occurrences (PMI 10.0). The angel of Rev 14:6 stands at the end of the basar-euangelizo chain — the vocabulary establishes continuity with OT good-news proclamation.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 1. The basar-euangelizo chain proves the gospel is not a NT innovation.

word_studies.summary: Patience/endurance/steadfast perseverance. Feminine noun. Used in both Rev 13:10 and 14:12, creating a literary bracket (hupomone inclusio) that binds the beast narrative to the angel messages as a single unit.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objections 4, 11. The hupomone bracket makes Rev 13-14 a single literary unit.

word_studies.summary: To fear/revere. Hebrew verb. 414 OT occurrences. Used for both terror (Gen 28:17) and reverence (Ecc 12:13). LXX collapses into phobeo. Exo 20:20 uses yare twice in one verse with opposite meanings: 'Fear not' (stop terror) AND 'that his fear may be before your faces' (maintain reverence).
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 5. The Hebrew root behind phobeo in Rev 14:7.

word_studies.summary: Sabbath/rest. Hebrew noun. The seventh-day rest. Called owth (sign) four times. Absent from Revelation by name — but the creation formula in Rev 14:7 alludes to the fourth commandment via the poieo/asah verb match.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 6. The Sabbath is NOT named in Revelation — honest acknowledgment required.

word_studies.summary: Fountain/spring/source. Feminine noun. Rev 14:7 substitutes 'fountains of waters' (pegas hydaton) where every other Exo 20:11 echo uses 'all that in them is.' This divergence marks Rev 14:7's allusion as both dependent on Exo 20:11 and deliberately modified.
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 6. The 'fountains of waters' divergence — Rev 14:7's unique fourth element.

word_studies.summary: Judgment seat/tribunal/platform. Neuter noun. Used in 2 Cor 5:10 for the judgment seat of Christ where all believers appear. An evaluative tribunal, not a condemnation court.
word_studies.relevance: Supporting Objection 8. Believers face the bema — evaluation, not condemnation.

word_studies.summary: To keep/guard/observe. Verb. 75 NT occurrences. Johannine concentration (John 18, 1 John 8, Revelation 11). Used in Rev 14:12 as terountes (present active participle) — 'those keeping.' The participle is descriptive (who the saints are), not prescriptive (conditions for salvation).
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objection 4. The participle terountes describes rather than prescribes.

word_studies.summary: Love. Feminine noun. The teleia agape (perfect/complete love) in 1 John 4:18 casts out punishment-fear. The faith-love-law chain: Gal 5:6 (faith works by love) → 1 John 5:3 (love IS keeping commandments).
word_studies.relevance: Core to Objections 4, 5. Love connects faith to commandment-keeping.
